title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a climate researcher called Emma Lowe talking about her recent expedition to the Arctic. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a climate researcher called Emma Lowe talking about her recent expedition to the Arctic. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Emma Lowe describes the scientific work and daily survival during a stay in a remote Arctic camp.
extracts.audio_script: Hi everyone. My name is Emma Lowe and I’ve just returned from a six-week research mission in the High Arctic. Our goal was to gather data on the rate of glacial melting. [7]I spent most of my time collecting ice core samples from the glacier surface[/7]. These samples act like a timeline, showing us the chemical composition of the atmosphere over thousands of years. We worked closely with a team back in Norway who [8]provided us with high-resolution satellite imagery to help us navigate the moving ice[/8]. It was essential for our safety. The weather was our biggest enemy. [9]I found that a period of heavy snowfall made our work almost impossible for three days[/9], as we couldn't even see the equipment outside our tents. Survival in such temperatures requires careful planning. [10]The camp was equipped with a series of solar-powered heaters to keep our sleeping quarters bearable[/10]. They were surprisingly efficient. Living in such a remote area has a strange psychological effect. [11]I was struck by the total silence of the Arctic night, which was both beautiful and terrifying[/11]. You feel very small in such a vast landscape. We did have some excitement, though. [12]The sudden appearance of a polar bear near our supply tent was a very tense moment[/12] for the whole team. We had to follow strict safety protocols until it moved away. My research confirmed some worrying trends. [13]I am convinced that changes in ocean currents are accelerating the loss of sea ice[/13] in this region. It’s a complex problem with global consequences. As the project leader often told us, [14]we are essentially reading the 'climate clock' before it runs out of time[/14]. It was a privilege to be part of such a vital mission.

dialogue.speaker: emma
dialogue.text: Hi everyone. My name is Emma Lowe and I’ve just returned from a six-week research mission in the High Arctic. Our goal was to gather data on the rate of glacial melting. I spent most of my time collecting ice core samples from the glacier surface. These samples act like a timeline, showing us the chemical composition of the atmosphere over thousands of years. We worked closely with a team back in Norway who provided us with high-resolution satellite imagery to help us navigate the moving ice. It was essential for our safety. The weather was our biggest enemy. I found that a period of heavy snowfall made our work almost impossible for three days, as we couldn't even see the equipment outside our tents. Survival in such temperatures requires careful planning. The camp was equipped with a series of solar-powered heaters to keep our sleeping quarters bearable. They were surprisingly efficient. Living in such a remote area has a strange psychological effect. I was struck by the total silence of the Arctic night, which was both beautiful and terrifying. You feel very small in such a vast landscape. We did have some excitement, though. The sudden appearance of a polar bear near our supply tent was a very tense moment for the whole team. We had to follow strict safety protocols until it moved away. My research confirmed some worrying trends. I am convinced that changes in ocean currents are accelerating the loss of sea ice in this region. It’s a complex problem with global consequences. As the project leader often told us, we are essentially reading the 'climate clock' before it runs out of time. It was a privilege to be part of such a vital mission.

questions.explanation: She notes that heavy snowfall made work almost impossible for three days.

questions.explanation: The camp was equipped with solar-powered heaters.

questions.explanation: The appearance of a polar bear near the supply tent was a tense moment.

questions.explanation: She is convinced that changes in ocean currents are accelerating ice loss.

questions.explanation: The project leader said they were reading the 'climate clock'.
